coredata.dc:title: Charge and energy transfer interplay in hybrid sensitized solar cells mediated by graphene quantum dots
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Mihalache, Iuliana
Radoi, Antonio
Mihaila, Mihai
Munteanu, Cornel
Marin, Alexandru
Danila, Mihai
Kusko, Mihaela
Kusko, Cristian
coredata.dc:description: We explored the role of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as co-sensitizers in hybrid dye sensitized solar cell (DSSC) architectures, focusing on various concurring mechanisms, such as: charge transfer, energy transfer and recombination rate, towards light harvesting improvement. GQDs were prepared by the hydrothermal method that allows the tuning of electronic levels and optical properties by employing appropriate precursors and synthesis conditions. The aim was to realize a type II alignment for TiO2/GQD/dye hybrid configuration, using standard N3 Ru-dye in order to improve charge transfer. When GQDs were used as co-sensitizers together with N3 Ru-dye, an improvement in power conversion efficiency was achieved, as shown by electrical measurements. The experimental analysis indicates that this improvement arises from the interplay of various mechanisms mediated by GQDs: (i) enhancement of charge separation and collection due to the cascaded alignment of the energy levels; (ii) energy transfer from GQDs to N3 Ru-dye due to the overlap between GQD photoluminescence and N3 Ru-dye absorption spectra; and (iii) reduction of the electron recombination to the redox couple due to the inhibition of the back electron transfer to the electrolyte by the GQDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
dye sensitized solar cell
co-sensitizer
charge separation
energy transfer

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of luminescent graphene quantum dots (GQDs), modification of TiO2 electrodes and cell preparation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) HR-TEM image of GQDs (inset: the corresponding fast Fourier transform (FFT) pattern); (b) X-ray diffraction pattern of GQDs; (c) Raman spectrum of GQDs.
(a) X-ray photoelectron survey spectrum; (b) high-resolution N 1s spectrum; (c) high-resolution C 1s spectrum.
Photoluminescence emission spectra of GQDs at different excitation wavelengths; inset graph: UV–Vis absorbance of GQDs.
Cyclic voltammograms for GQDs and reference curve of Pt electrode recorded in TBAP as supporting electrolyte using a freshly cleaned Pt working electrode.
Schematic presentation (a) and diagram of the energy levels of each component relative to the vacuum level (b) for the (GQD-N3)DSSC hybrid system.
J–V curves of DSSCs taken under 1 sun illumination intensity. (For interpretation of the references to colour in the text, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(a) UV–Vis spectra recorded for stock solutions of N3 dye and GQDs, respectively; (b) IPCE spectra of N3-DSSC and (GQD-N3)DSSC recorded at low incident light intensities.
Open circuit photovoltage decay plots of the DSSCs; Inset graph: electron response time with Voc.
Photovoltaic parameters of DSSCs obtained from J–V measurements.

head.ce:title: Charge and energy transfer interplay in hybrid sensitized solar cells mediated by graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Institute for Research and Development in Microtechnologies (IMT – Bucharest), 126A Erou Iancu Nicolae Street, 077190 Bucharest, Romania

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Institute for Research and Development in Microtechnologies (IMT – Bucharest)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Physics Department, University Bucharest, P.O. Box MG-11, 077125 Bucharest, Romania

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Honeywell Romania, Sensors and Wireless Laboratory-Bucharest, G. Constantinescu str. 3, 020339 Bucharest, Romania

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Physical Chemistry “Ilie Murgulescu”, 202 Spl. Independentei, 060021 Bucharest, Romania

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We explored the role of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as co-sensitizers in hybrid dye sensitized solar cell (DSSC) architectures, focusing on various concurring mechanisms, such as: charge transfer, energy transfer and recombination rate, towards light harvesting improvement. GQDs were prepared by the hydrothermal method that allows the tuning of electronic levels and optical properties by employing appropriate precursors and synthesis conditions. The aim was to realize a type II alignment for TiO2/GQD/dye hybrid configuration, using standard N3 Ru-dye in order to improve charge transfer. When GQDs were used as co-sensitizers together with N3 Ru-dye, an improvement in power conversion efficiency was achieved, as shown by electrical measurements. The experimental analysis indicates that this improvement arises from the interplay of various mechanisms mediated by GQDs: (i) enhancement of charge separation and collection due to the cascaded alignment of the energy levels; (ii) energy transfer from GQDs to N3 Ru-dye due to the overlap between GQD photoluminescence and N3 Ru-dye absorption spectra; and (iii) reduction of the electron recombination to the redox couple due to the inhibition of the back electron transfer to the electrolyte by the GQDs.

ce:section.ce:para: In solar technologies it is generally accepted that the dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSC) together with organic photovoltaics represent the third-generation of energy harvesting devices, bringing mainly the advantage of simplicity in their fabrication procedures, done practically under ambient conditions. One of the main drawbacks is related to the charge loss during the transport of photogenerated electrons in photoanodes.

The unique properties of carbon based nanomaterials (e.g. morphological, electrical, optical, and mechanical ones) made them a legitimate opportunity for enhancing the performances of both energy-conversion and storage devices [1]. Accordingly, intensive efforts were devoted to explore their feasibility as cheaper and eco-friendly materials to replace the components of conventional DSSC, like in the case of alternative counter electrode instead of platinum ones, whose high cost, rarity or risk of corrosion by the redox species are known, and therefore, the significant improvements brought by using carbon nanotubes (CNT) [2], graphene nanosheets [3] or carbon nanoparticles [4] were demonstrated. On the other hand, the incorporation of carbon-based nanomaterials in the photoanode seems to be considered very promising in terms of reducing the photoelectrons’ loss and improving its electrical conductivity [5,6]. However, the low surface contact between 1D (e.g. CNT) or 2D (e.g. graphene) nanomaterials with nanostructurated TiO2 film is seen as the main impediment for enhancing the device performance.

In the recent years, the graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have generated interest both in the fundamental science [7,8] and for potential applications in chemical sensing and bioimaging [9,10] or even as channels for field effect transistors [11] devices. Regarding the energy storage and conversion devices, the GQDs have been defined as an emerging material for energy-related applications and beyond [12] due to their extraordinary optical and electrical properties. Hence, their huge potential for memory devices [13], supercapacitors [14], fuel cells [15], photovoltaic devices [16,17], light-emitting diodes [18] was revealed. Furthermore, different architectures of solar cells based on GQDs have been proposed, where they form heterojunctions with crystalline silicon or silicon nanowires [19,20] and organic bulk heterojunction [21] on one hand, or they were embedded in polymers to replace the standard TiO2 photoanode [22] or Pt counter electrode [23] in DSSCs on the other hand.

In ultraviolet spectral range, GQDs have extinction coefficients ranging from 10 to 200·103 M−1 cm−1 [24], larger than that of common fluorophores, and comparable to those of semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) [25]. Due to this fact, GQDs can be physically included in a photoanode to act as a sensitizer in quantum dot - sensitized solar cells (QDSSC), but only very low efficiencies have been reported to this point, albeit different GQDs synthesis methods or different architectures of TiO2 electrode were proposed [26–29]. The explanation for the rather low efficiencies of the GQDs sensitized solar cells resides in the limited spectral range of absorption as well as in their low quantum yield compared with the standard fluorophores or semiconducting QDs.

A method to circumvent these issues is to use hybrid sensitizers containing both GQD and conventional dyes such that their absorption spectra cover the useful range of the solar spectrum. Several studies reporting on the interaction between semiconductor quantum dots and different organic dyes were published [30,31], but few data are available for GQDs – dye interaction [32,33]. The proposed explanation for the improved efficiency of hybrid solar cells co-sensitized with GQD and N719 Ru-dye is the injection of hot electrons from GQD into TiO2 photoanode [32] or the up-conversion properties of GQD [33]. However, for GQD’s sizes of several nanometers the photon flux challenge is not met [34] making unlikely for the solar radiation to cause multiphoton processes and generate hot electron populations. It is clear that a deeper understanding of the competition between various mechanisms such as charge transfer and energy transfer in hybrid systems is necessary in order to better exploit the special opto-electronic properties of GQDs.

An advantage of GQDs is the possibility to engineer their electronic properties such us gap and energy levels, by appropriate surface functionalization. In this way, when employed together with a conventional dye, one can obtain a charge transfer type II band alignment or an energy transfer type I band alignment. It is important to mention that the energy transfer process from GQD to dye can also occur in type II band alignment when there is a strong overlap between the PL emission spectra of GQD and the absorption spectrum of the dye [31].

Another salient feature of the GQD which is of interest for the sensitized solar cells is the capability of GQDs to store charge. As we observed in various types of electrical and photoelectrical measurements like, for instance, in time rising and decay photoconductivity measurements of GQDs embedded in polymers, the GQDs have a lifetime of tens of seconds [13]. This suggests an inhibited recombination mechanism in these systems. Starting from this finding, it would be of interest to investigate whether in other physical systems such as DSSC the presence of GQDs in the TiO2 layer will, eventually, act as inhibitors for recombination improving in this way the charge collection.

In this article we explore the GQD influence on the electrical parameters of hybrid sensitized solar cells that is both the dye and the GQDs are sensitizers. Firstly, by a simple one-step microwave assisted synthesis method we obtained GQD presenting an improved quantum yield as well as the desired bandgap and energy levels, appropriate for a type II band alignment for a hybrid GQD and standard Ru-dye sensitized solar cell, N3. Moreover, the as-prepared GQDs have a self-passivated surface, bearing amino functional groups, as shown by the XPS survey that improved their attachment capabilities on the titanium oxide surface. Secondly, in order to ascertain whether the GQDs played a role in improving the conversion efficiency, we realized and characterized a proof-of-concept solar cell using standard commercial DSSC components. We did not focus on optimization studies, in terms of dye and GQD impregnation times on TiO2 or additional treatments, our only interest being to investigate mechanisms underlying functionality of hybrid GQD based DSSC.

The effects of GQDs on the hybrid DSSC performances were investigated by means of current-voltage (I–V) measurements under dark and illumination conditions, incident photon to current conversion efficiency (IPCE) and open-circuit voltage decay (OCVD). We demonstrate that the presence of GQDs leads to a consistent improvement of the photocurrent and power conversion efficiency with respect to a conventional N3 based DSSC as shown by I–V measurements. The light harvesting observed for the hybrid structure of GQDs and dye was determined by a simultaneous fulfilment of several requirements: (i) extension of the spectral absorption range; (ii) energy transfer between GQDs and N3 dye; (iii) existence of a multistep cascade energy levels of the TiO2-GQD-N3 dye system; (iv) efficient spatial separation of the electrons and holes; (v) facilitation of the electron injection from dye to TiO2 and GQD; and (vi) longer recombination time.

d-(+)-Glucosamine hydrochloride, 2-Amino-2-(hydroxymethyl)-1,3-propanediol (Tris), ethanol and acetonitrile (ACN) were supplied by Sigma–Aldrich. Tetrabutylammonium perchlorate (TBAP) was provided by Molekula GmbH. Test cell spare parts, electrolyte and dye were bought from Solaronix. Commercially available drilled Pt electrodes prepared by screen-printing a platinum precursor on a TCO22–7 FTO coated glass (reference number: 74,201), gaskets (74,301), sealing (74,401), caps (74,501), Iodolyte AN-50 (31,121) and Ruthenizer 535 (N3) (21,552) were supplied without any modification. Customized transparent titania electrodes consisted of a barrier layer, a 10μm TiO2 mesoporous layer and a 4μm reflective layer were used in our experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of luminescent graphene quantum dots (GQDs), modification of TiO2 electrodes and cell preparation
ce:section.ce:para: The luminescent graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were synthesized by a microwave assisted single step hydrothermal route. A microwave acid digestion vessel (4781 type) provided by Parr Instrument Company and a commercial microwave oven were used during synthesis. In a typical procedure, 1.0g of d-(+)-Glucosamine hydrochloride and 6.9g of 2-Amino-2-(hydroxymethyl)-1,3-propanediol were solubilised in distilled water (10mL). The solubilisation process was accelerated using a laboratory ultrasounds bath (Elmasonic X-tra basic 300) for 25minutes. The entire volume was introduced in the reaction vessel and during 60seconds, 700W were applied; the reaction mixture was allowed to cool down at room temperature for 45minutes. The reaction mixture was further centrifuged at 20.000rpm, at 10°C, using a Beckman Coulter Avanti J-30I centrifuge. The supernatant was collected and used for further experiments or stored at 4°C. Functionalization of titania electrodes was achieved: (1) by liquid impregnation, using the graphene quantum dots supernatant solution, during 2hours immersion time; (2) by immersion in a 5·10−4 M Ruthenizer 535 (N3) alcoholic solution for 2hours staining time; (3) TiO2 electrodes were first immersed during 2hours in the GQDs supernatant solution and thereafter soaked for another 2 more hours in 5·10−4 M Ruthenizer 535 solution. After soaking in GQDs supernatant solution, the electrodes were rinsed with water and dried under a gentle flow of nitrogen and after immersion in Ruthenizer 535 solution the electrodes were cleaned with ethanol and dried under a N2 stream. Once the functionalization of the titania electrodes was achieved, the sealing and the filling of the cells were accomplished using the Solaronix Assembly Instructions.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurements were performed on FEI Tecnai™ G2 F30 S-Twin microscope at an accelerating voltage of 300kV. A small amount of carbon dots suspension was drop casted on holey carbon-coated copper grids and subsequently air-dried before TEM analysis.

The crystalline structure of the obtained graphene quantum dots was characterized by X-ray diffraction methods with a 9kW rotating anode Rigaku SmartLab thin films diffraction system using a parallel beam setup (wavelength λCuKα =1.541867Å, beam divergence 0.05°). The XRD measurements were performed using quartz capillary sample tubes, at room temperature, at U=45kV, I=200mA, goniometer step of 0.01 degrees, scan speed of 1.2000 deg./min. degrees, scan range 10 to 60 degrees, scan axis θ – 2θ (coupled scan). The X-ray data was fitted using the PDXL integrated powder X-ray analysis software packages (full version 2.1.0) from Rigaku Corporation Japan. Mean crystallite size and strain were estimated by various methods: the Halder–Wagner method (variant of Hall–Williamson method) and the Whole Pattern Profile Fitting WPPF method (Rietveld analysis). Lattice parameters refinement was performed with the same analysis software package.
The Raman spectrum was acquired with a LabRam HR800 Raman at the excitation wavelength of 633nm.

Surface analysis performed by X-Ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out on a Quantera SXM equipment, with a base pressure in the analysis chamber of 10−9 Torr. The X-ray source was Al Kα radiation (1486.6eV, monochromatized) and the overall energy resolution is estimated at 0.65eV by the full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the Au4f7/2 line. In order to take into account the charging effects on the measured Binding Energies (BEs), the spectra were calibrated using the C1s line (BE=284.8eV, CC (CH) n bondings) of the adsorbed hydrocarbon on the sample surface. A dual beam neutralizing procedure (e− and Ar+ ion beams) has been used to compensate the charging effect in insulating samples.

Fluorescence emission spectra were recorded at room temperature with an Edinburgh Instrument F920 spectrometer equipped with a 450W Xenon lamp as excitation source. The absolute fluorescence quantum yield of the GQD solution was determined using the integrating sphere setup. The fluorescence lifetimes were measured in time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) mode, using Nanosecond Pulsed Flash Lamp. Absorption spectra of the sensitizer solutions were recorded using a Hitachi U-0080D UV–Vis photodiode array spectrophotometer. The sensitised photo anode absorbance measurements were realized in diffuse reflectance mode using integrating sphere attached to the Edinburgh Inst. Fluorometer FLS920.

Electrochemical investigations were performed using an Autolab/PGSTAT302N electrochemical workstation, in 0.1M TBAP electrolyte prepared in ACN purged with nitrogen. Cyclic voltammetry measurements were performed using the classical three electrode configuration, i.e. a platinum disc working electrode, a platinum wire as counter electrode and Ag/AgNO3 reference electrode (RE-7 non-aqueous reference electrode from BAS Inc. Japan) in an acetonitrile solution containing 0.01M AgNO3 and 0.1M TBAP. Platinum (Pt) wire and disc electrodes (d=3.0mm) were purchased from Bioanalytical Systems.

The photovoltaic characteristics of the cells were measured using the PGSTAT302N potentiostat, while samples were illuminated with a broad band ozone-free Xe source through a standard AM1.5G filter, at a power density of 1 Sun (100mW/cm2) measured by a National Institute of Standards and Technology traceable thermopile detector. Moreover, to generate the incident photon to current conversion efficiency – IPCE (λ) – spectra, the F920 spectrometer’ monocromator was incremented over the 300–900nm wavelength range, and the short-circuit photocurrent density (mA/cm2) recorded with PGSTAT302N potentiostat; the Pin is the incident monochromatic light intensity (W/m2) measured with an optical detector and a power meter.

Fig. 1 (a) shows typical HR-TEM images of GQDs, with a size distribution ranging from 3.97 to 4.77nm, with an average diameter of ∼ 4.3nm. The crystallinity of GQD is clearly observable and the corresponding selected area fast Fourier transform (FFT) image shown in the inset of the Fig. 1 reveals a characteristic hexagonal diffraction pattern of the (100) facet of graphite, which further proves the formation of the graphitic structure. The lattice spacing of 0.252nm is consistent with the (1120) lattice fringe of graphene [35]. These observations are supported by the XRD pattern presented in Fig. 1(b), where three superimposed broad diffraction peaks are present, the main one centred at 27.6° with two shoulders at 21.60°, and at 38.89°, respectively. While the main peak corresponds to a lattice parameter of 3.23Å, close to the graphite (002) lattice spacing, the one at 38.89° corresponds to a d-spacing of 2.31Å, that could be assigned to a distorted (1120) graphite lattice with a compressive strain of △a/ao =− 9.1% [36]. The shoulder located at lower angles can be assigned to a minority phase (4.1Å) of a disordered (strained) carbon phase with a poor crystallization. The Raman spectrum of GQDs – Fig. 1(c) – shows two main peaks around 1335 (D-band) and 1590cm−1 (G-band), respectively. The G band (≈ 1580cm−1), is the main Raman signature in graphene related systems [37]. The D band (≈ 1350cm−1) is associated, in both graphene and single wall carbon nanotubes (SWNTs), to defective carbon-based areas, and its intensity is related to the crystalline size [38]. Tuinstra and Koenig [37] observed that the ratio of the D to G intensities varied inversely with the crystallite size. The intensity ratio of D- and G- bands (ID/IG) was calculated to be about 1.3, specific for few nanometers GQDs [39], reflecting the presence of structure defects, vacancies and edges in GQDs. The distance between defects in graphene, evaluated on the ID/IG ratio is roughly 1.65nm [40]. In unoxidized, single-layer graphene, the intrinsic defect concentration is very low, thus the disorder band (D) is barely detectable (ID/IG ≤0.01) [41]. However, the Raman results support the XRD and TEM analyses that the synthesized GQDs have crystalline structure features of graphene. The presence of oxygenated groups (CO or/and CO) and CN groups induced more defects and vacancies in the lattice of the GQDs, contributing to the alteration of lattice parameters, quantum yield efficiency and increase of ID/IG ratio [42].

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was further used to determine the chemical states of the elements present on the surface and, after quantitative analysis, to find out the elements and the chemical state relative concentrations, as well. After the scanning survey – Fig. 2 (a) – the high resolution photoelectron spectra of the most prominent XPS transitions (C1s, O1s and N1s) were recorded for the GQDs sample. It is appropriate to note here that all the calculations were performed assuming that the samples were homogeneous within the XPS detected volume (< 10nm). We have to emphasize that the errors in our quantitative analysis (relative concentrations) were estimated in the range of±10%, while the accuracy for binding energies (BEs) assignments was±0.2eV.

The high-resolution N1s spectrum of the GQDs – Fig. 2(b) – reveals the presence of both pyridine-like (398.0 eV, 61.1% molar ratio) and pyrrolic (400.4 eV, 21.2% molar ratio) N atoms [43]. A N1s peak of amino groups appears at 399.7 eV (17.7% molar ratio) suggesting the successful functionalization of the graphene quantum dot surfaces. C1s high-resolution spectrum – Fig. 2(c) – of the GQDs shows four main components, arising from sp2 (284.8eV, 60.8% molar ratio) and sp3 (285.8eV, 4.0% molar ratio) domains, COH (hydroxyl, 286.7eV, 4.5% molar ratio) and surprisingly from poly(carbazole) [44]. This implies that the majority of the C atoms in graphene were arranged in a conjugated honeycomb lattice and CN bonds were successfully achieved. The elemental relative concentrations in GQDs (at. %) were as follows: C1s (66.3%), N1s (11.7%) and O1s (22.0%). A chemical structure of GQDs was proposed based on XPS survey, as well as on Raman analysis (Fig. S1 – Supporting Information). The presence of various functional groups attached on the surface can easily influence the optical properties of GQDs [45] that, subsequently, can be improved by using a slight variation in the synthetic routes or molecular precursors. All the samples were investigated at room temperature and were monitored for a couple of months, demonstrating superior photostability which is essential for efficient optoelectronic devices. Typically, GQDs materials display broad bandwidths and excitation dependent photoluminescence emissions as their main spectroscopic features.

Fig. 3 (inset) shows the GQDs absorption spectrum ranging between 200 and 900nm. The primary peak at 270 nm was assigned to π–π* transitions of CO and aromatic CC bonds in sp2 hybridization region and a weaker shoulder centred at 400 nm is assigned to π–π* transition of C&9552;O bond ascribed to sp3 hybridization. Thus the absorption spectrum could also provide the fingerprints of graphene as the core of quantum dots structures. The agreement on carbon chemistry between both spectroscopic methods suggested the homogeneity of the samples with practically the same carbon chemistry in bulk and at the surface. This finding could explain the surface stability as it is well-known that the surface is subjected to different interactions with the environment components (oxygen, carbon dioxide, hydrocarbons, OH groups and even molecular nitrogen). Although absorption is recorded at all wavelengths in the spectrum, strong optical absorption appeared only in UV region with a tail extending in the visible range. Such wide absorption allows excitation with a spectrally wide light source, necessary to perform efficiently in further photovoltaic devices.

Photoluminescence emission is the result of electron-hole pair recombination. Fig. 3 shows a broad bandwidth covering wavelengths mainly from visible domain (350nm up to 600nm). PL emission has a constant bathochromic shift with a rapidly decreasing intensity whereas excitation was extending to longer wavelengths. Although the emission maximum changes according to Stock’s law, reaching a constant shift of 50 nm, the overall emission remains between the same boundaries of the visible spectrum around 400–700nm and further, an even more broad range is being expected for the GQDs film emissions. Also no modification of FWHM was observed for the recorded spectra. GQDs displayed a quantum yield of 7.42%, measured at 340nm excitation. This type of fluorescent behavior together with sufficient quantum yield (QY) may have a crucial role supporting the emerging of energy transfer phenomena for efficient co-sensitization of organic dyes. PL decay profile was recorded by time-correlated single photon counting technique combined with nanosecond pulse excitation of 340 nm (Fig. S2 – Supporting Information). The results showed two decay components: a fast decay one with a lifetime of 1 ns associated with a rapid recombination of excitons in the internal cores states and a slow decay one with a lifetime of 11ns associated with localized surface trap states [46]. The pH of GQDs solution had a marginal influence on GQDs photoluminescence (Fig. S3 – Supporting Information), hence the PL intensity varied by maximum 5% around the neutral pH value when exposed to acidic and alkaline environments.

The cyclic voltammetry measurements were used to probe directly the effects of confinement on the highest occupied molecular-orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular-orbital (LUMO) energy levels, and to find the defect/surface state levels [47]. Fig. 4 depicts the cyclic voltammograms of GQDs, as well as the behavior of Pt electrode in the presence of supporting electrolyte considered as blank reference, exclusively.

The onset – potentials of the oxidation and reduction peaks related to electron and hole injection into the conduction and valence bands were consequently used to determine the EHOMO (from Eox,onset) and ELUMO (from Ered,onset) [48] using the following two equations:
(1) EHOMO =−e (4.75+EOx,onset) (eV
(2) ELUMO =−e (4.75+ERed,onset) (eV
Self-passivated GQDs show a reduction peak at −1.75V and an oxidation peak at +1.14V (vs. Ag/AgNO3). The values for EHOMO, ELUMO calculated from these peaks' onsets are ELUMO,GQDs =− 3.16eV and EHOMO,GQDs =−5.68eV, respectively.

The schematic representation of the resulting DSSC, where both GQDs and N3 dye were used as sensitizers and the corresponding energy level diagram are presented in Fig. 5 . The cell photoanode consists of a mesoporous oxide layer (10μm), a network of TiO2 nanoparticles sintered together to establish the electronic conduction. Here the charge transfer dye and quantum dot sensitizers are interfaced with the TiO2 nanoparticles. Essentially, in a DSSC the electron excited into the LUMO state is injected into the conduction band of TiO2, while, simultaneously, the oxidized sensitizer is regenerated by the redox couple (I−/I3 −) present in the acetonitrile based electrolyte transferring the hole. The separated charges diffuse towards the contacts, where they are collected. The energy level diagram of the cell shown in Fig. 5(b) evidences the compatibility of the standard sensitizers used, N3, with the synthesized GQDs that act as additional light harvesters. Therefore, while the HOMO and LUMO level positions of the dye are at −4.98eV and −2.88eV, respectively [49], the GQD LUMO level is −3.16 eV and HOMO level was −5.68eV, corresponding to a type II alignment of the energy levels in hybrid systems [30]. The mismatch between the energy levels of the GQDs and those of N3 leads to a step-like band edge structure such that the electrons from the LUMO level of the dye descend to the titania electrode through the GQDs, enhancing the electron injection and charge separation [50].

A series of electrical measurements were employed to investigate the GQDs role in the QDSSC performances. Since identical Pt counter electrode and TiO2/TCO anode substrate were used for all the fabricated cells, the change at the interface TiO2/sensitizers is the only one responsible for the differences between the electrical/electrochemical parameters of the investigated cells.

To ascertain the role of GQD as sensitizers we performed a comparison between the electrical J–V characteristics taken at 1 sun illumination – see Fig. 6 – for the following configurations: (i) cells sensitized only with GQD (GQDSSC) – red line; (ii) cells sensitized only with N3 (N3-DSSC) – blue line; and (iii) cells with hybrid sensitizer consisting of GQD and N3 dye (GQD-N3)DSSC – green line. The bare TiO2 reference cell is represented with black line. The photovoltaic parameters of all cells obtained from these curves are listed in Table 1 .

Although the presence of amino functional groups on the GQD surface led to an improved affinity with the titanium oxide surface and, consequently, to a more satisfactory charge injection, the short circuit current density is still low (0.28mAcm2) for the GQDSSC cell, only marginally improved with respect to the current corresponding to the bare TiO2 cells. The open circuit potential (Voc) is 0.48V when GQDs were loaded on TiO2, a four time increase with respect to the reference cell. These values are in accordance with those of the previously reported GQD-based DSSCs [26–29]. Since the aim of our study was to investigate the interaction mechanism between the GQDs and N3 dye and their co-sensitisation influence on the DSSC performances, we did not focus on finding an optimized protocol to achieve higher efficiencies for the reference bare TiO2– and N3–DSSCs, respectively. It is well known that both the additional physical-chemical treatments of the TiO2 layer [51,52], and the tuning of dye concentration and immersion time [53] modulate the solar cell efficiency.

A significant improvement of the short circuit photocurrent density (∼ 18%) is observed for (GQD-N3)DSSC compared to N3-DSSC. The most obvious cause for this improvement is the enlargement of the range of the solar spectrum which is efficiently used for incident photon absorption in cells. Regarding the open circuit potentials (Voc), for the hybrid (GQD-N3)DSSC the Voc is 30 mV lower than the dye-cell, N3-DSSC. However, due to the larger short circuit current, the power conversion efficiency (PCE) – η – for the (GQD-N3)DSSC increases with 12%. It is notable that this enhanced efficiency was achieved for a simplified preparation protocol for the photoanode, where an identical short time of immersion was kept for the both GQDs and N3 sensitizers −2hours. This is similar with recently reported results obtained with GQDs and N719, although, in those cases the immersion times are significantly longer. For instance, in Ref. [32] the immersion time for GQD was 24h, and the one for dye was 12h. In Ref. [33] also, the immersion time for dye was 36h and the GQDs were deposited by spin coating.

The enhanced power conversion efficiency of the hybrid cell is consistent with the incident photon to current conversion efficiency (IPCE) curves of N3-DSSC and (GQD-N3)DSSC presented in Fig. 7 (b), where it is clearly shown that when titanium oxide was sensitized with both N3 and GQDs, the IPCE presents an enhancement over the entire range of visible spectrum. The interplay between several distinctive phenomena causes this enhancement.

Firstly, in UV range (wavelengths shorter than 380nm), an up to eight fold increase is observed. This effect is also supported by the UV–V is spectra recorded for solutions of N3 dye (2.5·10−5 M) and different volumes (50, 75 and 100μL) of GQDs presented in Supplementary Information – Fig. S4(a), where a remarkable increase in absorption relative to the pristine dye is observed when GQDs are added. Moreover, the UV–vis diffuse reflectance absorption spectra of the TiO2 photoanode subjected to each of the three forms of functionalization – with GQDs (GQDs/TiO2), and N3 (N3/TiO2), respectively, and with both GQDs and N3, successively, (GQD-N3)/TiO2, are presented in Fig. S4(b). While the pure TiO2 electrode exhibits no absorption in visible region, all the functionalized structures exhibit obvious light absorption in visible region, the most significant one being achieved for the hybrid (GQD-N3)/TiO2, which is roughly the sum of the individually sensitized films, GQD and N3 anchored TiO2, respectively. Notably, a slight broadening of the absorption peaks is observed when the molecules are adsorbed on TiO2 in comparison to the absorption characteristics in solution, Fig. S4(a), which also ensures a better light harvesting.

Furthermore, the absorbed photon to current efficiency (APCE) and light harvesting efficiency (LHE) were obtained using the photoanode abosorbances’ data, and the resulting spectra for N3-DSSC and (GQD-N3)DSSC are presented in Fig. S5 – Supporting Information. The APCE spectra show different features for (GQD-N3)-DSSC compared to the N3-DSSC, emphasising the modification of the recombination dynamics also observed from the Voc behavior.

An interesting feature is the difference between the IPCE spectrum of the hybrid cell and N3-DSSC in the spectral range 380–450nm, which cannot be associated with the rather flat increase of absorption. One can notice that while IPCE of the N3-DSSC presents a depression in this range, the hybrid cell exhibits a broad peak, with its maximum at 390nm. The ratio of the currents at this wavelength is approximately eight. To explain this difference, we put forward an energy transfer mechanism similar to the one proposed by Buhbut et al. [54], based on the comparison between the absorption spectrum of the colloidal GQD and the one of the N3 solution – Fig. 7(a) – with the IPCE curve – Fig. 7(b). As it can be seen in Fig. 7(a), in the spectral range 390–480nm the colloidal GQDs present an increase in absorption that leads to a corresponding significant increase in the IPCE.

For ascertaining the validity of this scenario we performed two types of measurements:
-
Firstly, the PL spectra of GQD functionalized TiO2 photoanode, revealed that in the UV region the PL is quenched while in the range 380–440nm, where the energy transfer might occur there is a sizeable PL (Fig. S6 – Supporting Information). The PL spectra in the range 380–440nm are broad and overlap the N3 absorption spectrum, achieving a prerequisite condition for energy transfer. Since the charge transfer depends on the density of states in TiO2 and GQD this leads to PL quenching in UV and a stronger PL at 380–440nm. Note that also the emission of colloidal GQD at λex =380nm overlaps the absorption spectrum of N3 as seen in Fig. S7 – Supporting Information.
-
Secondly, time resolved PL measurements realized at an excitation wavelength of 340 nm showed that the average time of the N3/TiO2 is 2.74ns, which is faster compared to the hybrid electrode – (GQD-N3)/TiO2 – where the average time is 2.84ns (Fig. S8 – Supporting Information). This suggests that a standard Forster resonance energy transfer (FRET) process is present in ultraviolet spectral range between the GQD donors and N3 dye acceptors.

Although the energy transfer is determined by a type I alignment, it was shown that it can also be present in type II alignment systems [31], as long as two conditions are satisfied: (i) a significant overlap between the emission spectra of the quantum dots and the absorption spectrum of the dye, and (ii) the gap of the GQD donor is larger than the gap of the dye acceptor [55,56]. Both conditions are fulfilled in (GQD-N3)DSSC. Moreover, according to cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurements the GQD LUMO is in close proximity to the N3 LUMO (an offset of 0.28eV). Also, CV probes the density of states, therefore there is the possibility that for short excitation wavelengths the exciton formed in GQD to be on higher levels such that the energy will be transferred to N3 via a standard type I alignment FRET process. A similar behavior was observed in the blue part of the spectrum in the case of other hybrid cells [54].

For longer excitation wavelengths, the exciton can be formed on the lower energy levels corresponding to the GQD surface states responsible for the excitation dependent PL. In this case the type II alignment is energetically favorable and the charge transfer becomes the dominant mechanism responsible for the IPCE enhancement. In this case, one has to investigate the GQDs role as mediators of charge transfer. The cascaded energy band alignment between the co-sensitizers facilitates the electron transfer and, consequently, a possible faster injection of electrons into TiO2. GQDs can also facilitate an improved charge collection, due to their longer recombination times as shown by the analysis of the dark I–V characteristics and Voc decay measurements. This picture suggests that there is a crossover between two coexisting mechanisms: energy transfer dominant process in the blue part of the spectrum and charge transfer towards the red part of the spectrum.

To confirm the kinetic parameters of the processes occurring on the sensitized photoanode and to separate the recombination behaviour at interface from other reactions, photovoltaic-decay measurements were realized and the plots of the transient open-circuit voltage after switching off the light (1 sun illumination intensity) are presented in Fig. 8 . Although the largest Voc value was obtained for the cell with only N3-dye, being slightly larger (15mV) than the cell with combined sensitizers, the photo-voltage decay reflects the rate of decreasing of the electron concentration caused by charge recombination [44] being a measure of the electron recombination rate at photo-anode-electrolyte interface. It successively decreased for GQDSSC> (GQD-N3)DSSC>N3-DSSC, which implies that GQDs capture the electrons and prohibit the electron back transfer. Moreover, to quantify the decay rate, the decay time constants were calculated from reciprocal time derivative normalized to thermal voltage equation [57]:
(3)
τ = −
k
B
T
e
(
d
V
OC
dt
)
− 1

The plots of the electron lifetime obtained from Eq. (3) as a function of voltages (Fig. 8 – inset) indicate clearly that the GQDs generated a significant increase of the decay-time constant, this being with approximately one order of magnitude higher for (GQD-N3)DSSC compared with N3-DSSC cell.

In search for the phenomenon responsible for this improvement, it would be of interest to know the dominant recombination mechanism in these cells. In this respect we calculated the absolute (Mulliken) electronegativity of the dye, with the formula (HOMO+LUMO)/2 [58,59]. The electronegativity of N3 dye is −3.93eV, well above the TiO2 conduction band margin. This implies that the dominant recombination mechanism in the N3-based cell is the electron interception by electrolyte. Therefore, the observed improvement in the lifetime can be associated with the inhibition of the back electron transfer to the electrolyte by the GQDs. To evidence the presence/absence of this mechanism, the dark current-voltage characteristics of the cells have been investigated. Fig. 9 shows that the dark current of the cell realized with the GQD co-sensitization is better than the one of the cell with N3 only.

This is a strong hint that the GQDs act as inhibitors for the electron recombination with the iodine/iodide redox couple. A similar effect has been reported in QDSSC [60] for a single-walled carbon nanotube layer deposited on indium-doped tin oxide electrode (ITO). However, when compared to CNTs, which are localized on the ITO electrode, in our case, the GQDs are distributed in the whole TiO2 matrix. In future, the efficiency of the GQDs based QDSSC can be further increased amending the photoanode properties by modifying the GQDs/dyes loading periods or using a supplementary chemical process to improve the physical adsorption or by using different thicknesses/morphologies of the TiO2 layer.

In summary, we developed a simple, and low-cost method to obtain GQDs with improved photoluminescence and desired electronic properties. The obtained GQDs were used as sensitizers in DSSC as well as co-sensitizers in (GQD-N3)DSSC. When the DSSC was sensitized with GQDs solely, the efficiency was low, presenting a small short circuit current (Isc =0.28mAcm2) and a rather large open circuit voltage (Voc=0.48 V), at 1 sun illumination. The low efficiency of the GQD based DSSC is attributable to the fact that GQD absorb mainly in the UV range of the solar spectrum, with a rather low absorption tail in the visible. However, the larger Voc in comparison with bare TiO2 DSSC is an indication that the GQD played an important role in charge separation and collection. This conclusion is supported by the increased efficiency obtained for hybrid (GQD-N3)DSSC, where N3 has the role of efficient absorber over the visible spectrum and the GQD realized a cascaded type II alignment of the energy levels. We evidenced by Voc decay measurements and dark I–V characteristics that GQDs are capable to reduce recombination of electrons with redox couple.

The IPCE analyses suggested that there could be also an energy transfer mechanism which significantly increases the photocurrent in the blue part of the spectrum. This hypothesis was confirmed by both steady state and transient fluorescence measurements of the sensitized photoanode. Moreover this possible process was supported by the significant overlap between the emission spectra of the quantum dots and the absorption spectrum of the dye, as well as by the fact that the gap of the GQD is larger than the gap of the dye. Consequently, the GQDs can act as donors in the presence of N3 dye.

This is an indication that the GQD play a dual role in the hybrid DSSC system leading to the improving of the light harvesting efficiency and the charge transfer properties. We emphasize that the cells were not optimized in terms of immersion times, additional treatments, etc. the aim of this work being to obtain a proof-of-concept device in order to investigate the GQD capabilities of light harvesting. Based on these results, it is reasonable to assume that GQD are effective co-sensitizers as mediators for light harvesting in hybrid DSSC.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: I. Mihalache acknowledges the support of the strategic grant POSDRU/159/1.5/S/137750. C. Kusko acknowledges the support of CNCS-UEFISCDI Romania, under the project number PNII-ID-PCCE-0069/2011.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.electacta.2014.11.200.
